So, for but for us at the scalable company, we're gonna go first. Our purpose is to help entrepreneurs scale themselves so that they can scale their companies, right? We fundamentally believe that companies are better, that teams are better. that families are better, that whole world economies are better when founding entrepreneurs stay at the helm of their companies as long as they want to.
Now, if one day you want to sell, go kick it on a beach one day, that's great. We want that for you as well. What we know is that that's not going to happen unless an operating system is in place, right? You want to run your company forever. You want to hand it off to the next generation. And that's great. We want that for you too. We know that none of that's going to happen unless an operating system is in place. So whatever you want. from your business, whatever your business goals are. Um, the work that we're doing here is going to position you do it, and in between now and then. It's just going to help you have a better life. And that's what we want for you.
Because I've been there. I've been that entrepreneur who got burnt out. I've been that entrepreneur who fired myself. I've known plenty of entrepreneurs who got fired from their own companies by a board that no longer believed they had what it took. I hate that. I don't want that to ever happen to an entrepreneur. You want to leave on your own terms? Great. Nobody should force you out, least of all yourself, right? Least of all yourself. And so how do we do that, right? The way that we do that is by building operating systems, right? And we specifically say we want to install a business operating system so your company can run and scale without you. Right. So your company can. run in scale without you.
And the emphasis there is the word 'can.' It doesn't have to. But knowing that it can. Knowing that your business doesn't own you, you own your business. We believe that that is what is going to make all the difference, not just to how quickly your business can grow, not just how much your business can grow, but to how much you can enjoy it during the process. Because you deserve to enjoy the process. Now, the reason that we're here is because this... world that we are in, that we have all chosen, that we've been called into, this entrepreneurial world is a roller coaster. Right. And we know that it is a roller coaster. And what we've seen is that time and time again, no matter how many entrepreneurs I talk to—those who are literal billionaires to entrepreneurs who are just getting started making their first few sales—what we see is that this pattern that I'm going to show you here, this pattern is just universally true.
Every entrepreneur I've ever met has experienced some version of this pattern, and it all starts with that eureka moment. Right? That moment. when you have that idea. Now, what makes you different? from most people, because lots of people have ideas, right? You acted upon it. I can't tell you how many times I've talked to, you know, frustrated friends who were like, 'Oh, you know, I had the idea for the Snuggie.' I mean, give me a break. It's just a blanket with arms. I could have done that too. Yeah, but you didn't. You didn't. Lots of people have ideas, but not a lot of people are willing to kind of power through that early drudgery. when it feels hopeless. Right?
When the idea that you had that made so much sense, you know, it doesn't stand up to early scrutiny or your first couple of versions don't work out or that first couple of clients don't quite get the value. Hope that they would. But all of you powered through to that initial point of traction. Where you say, 'Wait, we might actually have something here.' And then something really cool happens, right? After that initial point of traction, we push on. And we get this sense of momentum, right? It's working. It's working. And we declare it's time to scale. We're all going to be rich. And for whatever reason. At our peak entrepreneurial optimism and enthusiasm. It is at that moment when things start to flatline.
It is at that moment we say, 'That's odd.' Where'd all the growth go? It's at that moment where we say, 'Seriously, why isn't this working?' And it's at that moment, at some point, when we ultimately declare, 'That's it. I'm a failure, and we are all doomed.' And so what I want you to know is that if you're there right now, if you're in that place where you're feeling like a failure, if you're in that place where you're almost at peak imposter syndrome. All those fears that you had of not being worthy, of not being truly ready to run your business at the next level. If you are in that place, I want you to know that you're in the right place. You are not broken. Your business isn't broken.
Simply means that the operating system that got you here needs an upgrade. Now, let's say you're not at that place. Let's say you're earlier on in the cycle. Maybe you're just past traction. You're experiencing that early growth. What I want you to know is that if you continue to operate on what we call a UOS, you will get here. Okay, you will absolutely flatline out. Right. And if you don't believe that. Right, you just look at some of the heads not on the screen, like, trust me, like you will definitely get there. Which means you're also in the right place, because maybe, just maybe, you can avoid this flat line. Maybe, just maybe, you can keep it going up and to the right.
But that only happens if you have an upgradable operating system, which is what we're building. So, when we think about the entrepreneurial life cycle, we break it down into a couple of phases or zones, right? There's that initial launch zone. That time from idea to traction this is what you know. This is why most you know would be entrepreneurs are actually entrepreneurs— they want to be an entrepreneur, but they never get the traction. They never get through the launch zone. Then there's the growth zone. This is the most cherished and favorite part of any entrepreneurial journey when everything feels like You know, it's great when we can do no wrong. Yeah, systems are constrained, but it's fine. Everything's going to be fine. We'll figure it out. And then, at some point, it's not fine.
Right, it's not fine. Those systems that were held together by, you know, duct tape, bubble gum, and hope to come apart. And this is when we can start feeling stressed out, right? Stressed out because we can feel the weight of the entire company on us. Right. Um. Are you there right now? Like, drop in the chat. And I want us to do this. I want this to be interactive. You know, are you feeling stressed? I want you to think about your emotional state right now. Where are you right now? Was it a little bit stressful even showing up? Because you're like, 'I don't know if I got time.' Go to this thing today. I'm stressed. I got crap to do. Right?
And I want you to share that because I want everybody to, I want you to see that you're not alone. I want everybody else to see that they're not alone either. Is anybody out there stressed, Kyle? Maybe you feel pretty good about stuff. Maybe you're even still enjoying it. But you're like, when is this gonna like get easier? You're looking back on a time when things were so simple. Right? You remember when things were so simple? Because you didn't have— you know, a lot of these pesky customers or clients running around. Remember when things were so simple because you only had a single product offering? Remember when things were so simple because you didn't really have any employees or contractors? You were all you needed and you knew you. Right. So sometimes we just feel—exhausted. Uh. The point that I really want to encourage you is if you're feeling this way. If you're just feeling trapped. Because in the beginning, we can start to get stressed.
And then we can start to get exhausted. And all of those emotions, we can see a lot at the end of the tunnel. Right. It's when you start to feel trapped. And if you're there right now, you— in particular— I want to encourage you. You. If you are feeling trapped, feel like you're trapped in a business that you can't escape because you are the business, I want to encourage you. There is a way out and you are in the right place. And look, this is not a sales pitch. You're here. You bought. Okay. This is me encouraging you because I want to make sure that you don't give up during this process because you're in the right place and you are doing the right things.
The reason that we feel trapped is because, when we launch our business, we're operating on that you operating system. You are the operating system and the operating system is you. You're everything. And in the beginning, that was perfect. It was enough. Anything more would have been overkill. It would have been bureaucracy. It got you where you are and it was great and we can be thankful for it. Now what we need is a scalable OS, right? We need an operating system that can scale as your business scales and it doesn't require you to scale. Because in the beginning, right, that you operating system, that planet you. Torsdagsforskning. That was enough to launch that little business. And in the early days, that was enough to keep that little business in orbit.
But if you know anything about the solar system, if you know anything about... astrophysics, right? What we know is that little things orbit around big things. Little things orbit around big things. Moons orbit around planets. Planets orbit around stars. Stars are floating around the middle of black holes in galaxies, which are orbiting around who knows what. Right. But you don't have to be a physicist to know that little things orbit around big things. Which is why, when your business was little, it could orbit around you. You could keep it in orbit. But as your business grew, the opposite happened. Your business started flinging you around. So the reason I want you to be encouraged, this isn't just some false Pollyannic optimism.
Right, if you're feeling trapped, if you're feeling in this state where your business owns you and not the other way around, if you're experiencing kind of that entrepreneurial, you know, blue screen of death. Right, don't panic— this is a GIF. Don't think that something broke on your computer. But if you're here, if you're in full-blown system overload, I want you to be encouraged because you would only be here if you had done a lot of right things. Right? I hope you heard that. That's a writer downer. You would only be here. You would only be feeling this way. If your business had grown. In other words, you've been a victim of your own success. Which means you've been successful. So I want to encourage you. Right?
If you feel, if you could check any of these boxes, so mentally. Right? Mentally. Like, I want to go through these things and I want you to mentally check these boxes. If there's one of them that particularly calls out for you, drop it in the chat. But if you feel like your plans are loosely defined and rarely followed. You're trying, you're making plans. Maybe you're doing annual plans. Maybe you're doing quarterly plans. You're meeting with your team regularly. You plan, nothing happens. Right? You're not alone. There's a good chance that it's not that you're all just horrifically disorganized. It's a good chance that you're a victim of your own success. If work is chaotic. If nothing important ever seems to get done. Aside from those things that are on fire, it doesn't mean that you're broken.
It doesn't mean that your business is broken. It doesn't even necessarily mean that your team is broken. Right? It simply means that your business has grown. The operating system that you had has reached overload. If your team members need to constantly be told what to do. In particular, those team members who should know better. If you feel like you had people on your team who, like, six months ago, a year ago, two years ago, seemed really smart. And for whatever reason, they just keep getting dumber. Does that relate to anybody else? You have these people on your team. You're like, 'I don't know what happened.' They're really great. And now they're kind of dumb. They generally had stuff dialed in and now. They don't even, you know, brush their teeth without asking permission first, right?
What happened? There, there's a good chance that they're not like— at least not permanently broken. Right there's a good chance that they're just dealing with the same thing you're dealing with: system overload, operating system overload. You feel like your meetings are a waste of time. Right. You're just meeting and then you're meeting each meeting. You're talking about having another meeting. Not necessarily broken system overload. This is a biggie if your profits are low, even on higher sales. Maybe you're looking at it and saying, 'The most frustrating thing of all is we're actually growing.' We're making more money. We're just keeping less. Does that resonate with any of you? Are any of you generating more money now, revenue-wise, top-line-wise, generating more revenue now than you were 12, 24, 36 months ago, and yet you're actually keeping less?
And you're thinking, that doesn't make any freaking sense. If I'm making more, I should be. Making more. And yet, you're generating more, keeping less. You're not broken. Your business is broken. Your model isn't necessarily broken. It just means that your operating system is likely overloaded. If no one seems to know what the heck is going on, and worst of all, again, if you burned out and ready to quit. I want you to know if you're mentally checking some or all of these boxes. You're in the right place and the solution that we have here is the solution that you need. All right, you're absolutely in the right place. It's the solution that you need. Um, What I can tell you is that if you don't. take the actions that we're going to talk about.
If you continue to persist, in this thing. Right? Flat lines don't stay flat forever. They don't. Flat lines do not stay flat forever. And that's when we see businesses hit that kind of MOI, that kind of that moment of inflection, that inflection point. Right and you know you're there when you're at that burnout point, right? It's that moment where you're going to make a decision. Either something is going to change, or that's it— it's going to go down now. In the beginning, you're in what we call the pivot zone. And if you're kind of in that early stage, where things are starting to dip, you don't need to panic necessarily, but just know you need to take drastic, massive, intentional action. Massive intentional action.
Now, if you're flatline and you're like, 'That's okay, maybe we can hang around in this kind of flatline state for a while,' you can't. Flat lines always either go up or down. They never stay flat for long. And eventually, all businesses reach a point where there is no more polling. You're in the death zone. You're a zombie company, dead company walking. Right. I don't believe any of you were there right now. If you're here right now, you got enough hope. Minimum, you're in the pivot zone. Where we want you to be is in what we call that scalable zone. And to get there, it's simply about replacing that you operating system with a scalable operating system so that as your business grows, your operating system keeps it in orbit.
This is really important. I want you to hear this. This is not about making you better. This is not about making you more efficient. This is not about making you more productive. This is not a time management training. This is not personal development. All right. This is about getting you the heck out of the center of it, because at the end of the day, we want our business to be bigger than us, which means that we're no longer in it. We want you to operate what we call 'over the business.' You still have all the control. You're just no longer the one pulling all of the levers. That's when you're fully in the scalable zone. Again, I said before: if you're in that growth zone and you're here early, Congratulations.
I mean, if you can avoid the constraint zone altogether and just go straight from growth into scalability. I mean, this is like the kid that starts investing when he gets his first job at 16. Right turns around and is like, 'Mid 30s' and like, 'I have a couple million dollars through compounding'— and all the old people like, 'Why didn't I do that?' Right. And so, if you're in the growth zone, just know that those of us who are living in the constraint zone or who are burnt out— We're looking at you. We're thinking, 'You better freaking do this.' You better do this. Do not wait until it's too late. Don't even wait for the pain. Make the investments now in yours and your own company's scalability because it will pay dividends.
That is why we're doing this. That is what we are all about. Let's talk about how it is going to work. This is the definition of an operating system. That we're going to keep referring back to again and again and again. This is the definition of the operating system that really drives kind of the overarching framework that we have. And that's just that an operating system is a set of algorithms and a common language. That enables the different components to communicate with one another. In the support of the desired outputs of a machine. In other words, an operating system is three things. Right, it's the set of algorithms— these are all your processes and sops. Because currently, all those things are stuck in your head. Right?
All your set of algorithms are likely stuck in your head or the heads of a couple of insiders. Common language? That's your communication, your alignment, your scorecard, your meeting rhythm, things like that. Because maybe right now you're just playing like a really bad version of the telephone game. Right. And then, of course, the desired outputs, you know, those are things like your goals and objectives. We just need to get more specific. We need to get clearer because at some point your desired objectives just become something along the lines of. you know, grow more than we did last month and don't run out of money. Right. Those kind of goals and objectives, those are enough in the early days. We need a bit more now.
So when it comes to upgrading your operating system, the three core steps, like the three big chunks of the build phase, are number one, we need to document the set of algorithms that make your business run. That's where it's all going to begin. Again, we do not start with. What's your purpose? What's your mission? What are your values? All those things are important. None of them are going to help what you're going through today. They're all kind of playing business. They're all organizing the file drawers. And if you've gone through that kind of work before and came out and realized. Look, we've got this pretty thing. Let's frame it or put it on a plaque and realize it will crash. Sales still aren't where they need to be. And I'm still freaking miserable.
That's why we don't start there. Okay. That's why we start with documenting the set of algorithms that make your business run. How do you create value today? How do you create value for your customers? How do you create value for you? We're going to document that. We're going to make sure that more of it can happen. Step two is: we need to define and develop your company's communication, architecture, and systems. Systems are great. At the end of the day, people need to run those systems. You're going to need people to run those systems, whether even if you are the only person— which by the end of this, but no matter what, people, outsourcers, somebody is going to need to run these systems. That's where your communication architecture, the scoreboards, those dashboards, how do we know how we're progressing?
And then, what's our meeting rhythm? We're going to talk exactly about how you should be structuring your meetings at your companies. A lot of people say, 'I don't want to have any meetings. I hate meetings. Meetings suck.' If your meetings suck, if they're not the most productive time that you're spending as a company, we're going to fix that. You're going to actually look forward to your meetings. And then, step three, we need to clarify your company's desired outputs. Define those goals, define those objectives, define what the culture looks like at your company. All that stuff is important. It just shouldn't be the first thing that we do. We shouldn't define goals based on the constraints that we have today. It's really important.
Don't define your goals based on what you know to be an overload or even overloaded or even broken operating system. We're going to fix all that, then redefine our goals. So these three components, right— the set of algorithms, the common language, the desired outputs— these are what form the basis of this. scalable operating system, which this is the overarching framework that we're going to be going through. This is the overarching framework and these six tools, right? Your value engines. How we begin to document the value creation process, your company playbooks. The checklist, standard operating procedures, and the high output team canvas, that thing that finally says who is responsible for what. These three tools are what combine to form the set of algorithms, the source code that is your operating system.
Company scorecards, your meeting rhythm, this is your user interface, this is your common language, and then the sixth— and I would say, probably most important— tool your clarity compass. This is what is going to once and for all clarify and define your desired outputs. All these six tools, this is what is going to allow you to climb that mountain, whatever it may be. All right, so these are the six tools that we're going to be building together over the next, again, kind of eight to 12 weeks. Now, in terms of the program itself. Let's talk about how we're going to make this happen, what we're going to be doing. And to kick off, we'll talk about how this is different from maybe other programs you might have gone through.
Number one, this is not just a course. Okay, you can treat this as a course if you want and still get a ton out of it. There are plenty of people who go through these programs. They just watch the videos. They get a lot out of it. That's great. But what makes this particular program different is it's cohort-based. Meaning we are all starting today. We're going to be going through progressing together. So you're not alone. All right. And best of all, you get to copy off your neighbors. We're going to be sharing our work. Here's what I got going on with this. We'll share some of ours and we hope that you'd be willing to share some of yours as well. Also, it's project-based. This is not just about learning.
It's not just about knowledge. Yes, we hope you learn a lot. Yes, we hope you gain some critical insights and information. This is about getting stuff done, though. All right, you're going to start today with a UOS, you will finish with a scalable OS, and we're going to clearly define what that is, right? So these kind of six tools are going to be six mini projects. That all combine to form it. We're going to install it together. We're going to get stuff done. We will have tangible work completed by the time we're finished. I think one of the most powerful things is that we're all here with the same shared goal. There are businesses here that are brand new. There are businesses here that have been active for 20, 30, plus years.
There are businesses that are more brick and mortar. There are businesses that are 100% online. They're all different kinds of businesses— B2B, B2C. You name it. What makes this work, though, is the fact that we're all here, we're all aligned to the same goal, the same objective. And that's going to give us a common language. And then the fourth way that this is different is you get massive support. Again, this is not just a course that you go through. And if you have some questions or you lose your login. And you email into a support, and they send you your login and they're like, 'Good freaking luck.' Okay. You are surrounded by a team of mentors, advisors. All right. We are all here to help.

Now, those of you who are familiar with kind of our broad overarching framework, the seven levels of scale. This will be a little bit of view, but these are the seven levels. That all companies must go through to progress from that struggling startup to this highly profitable, even exitable business that allows you to hit your number.
Right? Level one, sell and serve 10. Level two, get that growth flywheel built. Good news, we're going to touch on that. Even better news, you got to... Correct me if I'm wrong, Kyle. So that level two, the scalable growth accelerator, which you received as a bonus. It's all about getting to and through level two. I'm going to encourage you to pause on that one. What we found is that people who go through the Scalable OS Accelerator. Um, they want to go back and and rebuild their growth flywheel based on this new upgraded operating system, but this program is all about level three creating, rolling out, installing that upgraded operating system.
And it is at this point when you pass what we call the scalable line. Right at this point. Achieving level seven, meaning hitting your number. All right, achieving level seven, hitting your number. We truly believe that once we are done here, that will just be a matter of time. It will not be a question of if, it will simply be a question of when. And that's not just theory. Again, that's not just hyperbole. That is because we've identified that there are eight reasons that businesses get stuck and fail. Businesses get stuck and fail due to a lack of clarity. Nobody knows what the heck is going on or why they're doing it. A lack of demand. Not enough people wanting the thing that you're selling. Poor strategy— not executing or selecting the right projects, poor systems.
Right, inadequate systems so it's all dependent upon people doing the right thing perfectly every time. Which, oh yeah, they only never do. Not having the right team. Right people in the wrong seats, wrong people in the right seats or otherwise. Poor communications. Not enough capital. Misalignment between the entrepreneur, the goals of the entrepreneur and the goals of the company. These are the eight. reasons that good companies fail. These are the eight reasons that good companies get stuck. And I will submit to you that. You probably can't come up with another thing that isn't covered by one of these eight categories, right? These are the eight reasons that businesses get stuck and fail. And the beautiful thing is and why I can say with confidence that by the time we're done.
You hitting your number, you achieving your vision of the level seven life. The reason I can say that it's just a matter of. when not if is because you will have clarity. In fact, you will have a clarity compass. You'll have demand. In fact, you will have a growth flywheel, you know, fully documented. growth engine that's spinning like crazy. Demand won't be an issue. Strategy. You're going to have not just a meeting rhythm, but you're going to know how to execute quarterly sprints. You're going to have, frankly, in just a minute, a 12Q plan. Right? So strategy is not going to be an issue. Systems, you're going to have systems coming out of your ears. You're building an operating system, crying out loud. Team, you're going to have the high output team canvas.
You're going to have better documentation. Communication, again, that meeting rhythm. Scorecards, dashboards, capital. Very often we feel like we need capital when in fact what we need is cash flow. Right? You don't need capital. Your business can probably be self-funding. Let's get that operating system in place, get it operating with maximum efficiency. A good chance your capital needs. Growth capital needs can be funded out of greater efficiencies within the business that you already have. But I can assure you this: you want to go raise capital through either debt or equity. It's going to be a lot easier if you have an operating system in place. And then lastly, impact alignment. This simply means that the goals of the company, the goals of your customers, and your personal goals as the founder get out of alignment.
This is when we get burned out. This is when we quit. This is when we fire ourselves. You got an operating system in place when it's no longer a UOS— you can achieve impact alignment. So again, I want to encourage you. The work that you're doing is work that matters. It's work that's going to be leveraged. So let's begin that work. Let's officially get started. This is not going to take long. I know we're over just a bit. But we want to go ahead and get this process started now with beginning to do some initial strategic planning. All right, we're going to begin doing some initial strategic planning. Funny enough, the work that we're going to start here now, today, in the next couple of days, you know, in the next minute or so, this is where you're also going to finish.
So when you do your two-day planning offsite, the work that we do today, you're going to repeat with your leadership team. So I'm going to show you how to do this, you know, use this very simple tool. You don't have to share it with your team yet. You're certainly not finalizing it yet. But I want to give you a picture of what I believe the next three years could look like. Three-year target, what your number will be. And so to do that, there's kind of two shifts in our planning that I want to throw out there. The first, I want you to stop thinking in terms of annual planning. In my experience, most entrepreneurs either do or they say they do annual planning. And I truly believe that annual planning is one of the worst units of measurement.
The annum, the year, is one of the worst units of measurement there could ever be to create a plan. A year, 12 months, it is too long to be predictable and it is too short to be meaningful. It's too long. Who knows what's going to happen? You know, over the next six to 12 months. At the same time, how much can you really get done in a year? I just think it's kind of caught in this in-between, messy middle zone. So we don't like it. Um, annual planning. We also don't like end-of-the-year planning. In my experience, there could not be a worse possible time to plan than December. January. You're at peak distraction because it's the holidays. You're also at peak hopefulness. I like to say this is the equivalent of going grocery shopping when you're hungry.
Right. And to prove this, I want to ask. Those of you who did annual planning. How many of you are just right on track? I mean, here we are coming up on the end of the first quarter. First quarter's over. You nailing it? You just crushing life? Still just, you know, rocking that plan? If you are, that's great. But. I know from both experience and just some basic research. I think the number is 84%. 84% of people abandon their New Year's resolution by the end of February. Right? 84% of people abandon their New Year's resolution by the end of February. I also, uh, believe, although I don't have the data to support it, that about 84% of businesses, if not more, abandoned their annual plan by the uh end of March.
Um, anybody out there, Kyle, um, saying that, like, Yeah, no, they're right on track, crushing it, or. Most people saying, like, yeah. Might be off track. We have a totally agree from Jim. Melissa agrees. Steve, we plan annual numbers and sales goals. Dean says, just finishing up the stuff that I plan for Q2 2021, if that counts. And Simone says, I thought it's only me with a winky face. Yeah, well, so Steve. Decided that you're ahead of pace. I really am. That's awesome. I hope we can achieve more efficient moving forward. And for literally everyone, not Steve, because clearly Steve is better than us. Well, I'll admit it. I'll say it. It's, uh... You know, we're going to make sure that we have something that'll scale for you as well.
So all this kind of begs the question: it can be a little bit frustrating. I guess the other reason why I don't like annual planning is we feel like now we have to wait— another nine months to plan again. Right. And you don't. You don't, right? If you get away from annual planning and you shift to quarterly planning. And if you get away from setting an annual goal and you shift to setting a three-year target. So, because we really have found that for us. The annual is no good. We set three-year targets. Because you can accomplish a lot in three years. and we set and we do quarterly sprints, so three-year targets when we're doing our planning and we executed quarterly sprints. We plan in three-year cycles. We execute in quarterly sprints.
Three years, quarters. All right, so when is the next best time to plan for the next three years? I believe that that time is now. now is the absolute best time, even though it's kind of at a weird point in the year. Now is the best time. To plan for the next three years. You don't have to wait until you know, next December or January to do, we can do it now. And so the question I want to ask you is just how much do you plan to grow? As you sit there right now. What does three years, where do you want to be? In three years. Right, 36 months. 12 quarters from today. Three years from today. Where do you want to be? In terms of revenue and profitability.
Where do you want to be in terms of revenue and profitability? Now, in my experience, a lot of folks don't know. Or they don't know how to know the answer. So here's some general benchmarks that you may find helpful. When a Silicon Valley startup raises capital and they're going for that unicorn valuation, that hundred millions in revenue and a billion dollar valuation. They're... General growth target needs to be triple, triple, double, double. Okay. You know, after the first years, they kind of had a year of testing. They raised some money. Then they need to triple. They need to triple again. They need to double. They need to double. If they can do this and then kind of grow at 75 to a hundred percent, they will be at that $100 million valuation in five to seven years.
This is really difficult to do if you're not in a truly disruptive industry and you're not VC backed. Okay, so I wouldn't necessarily recommend that you plan on a, you know, to triple next year, triple the year after that, and then double the year after that. Really tough to pull off. Now, rapid growth. The double-double 75%— in terms of the next three years, that is something that is achievable if you're in the early stages of your business. All right, so if your business is fairly new, this can be very doable. Certainly, if you did $100,000 last year, it's not unrealistic to think that you could do $200,000 this next year, $400,000 the year after. right? These are all things that are very doable.
If you did 10 million last year, a hundred million last year, well, double, double 75% can be tough. So rapid growth. You know, good for funded companies, also good for early stage companies. That steady growth, 25% to 50%, that's kind of where most entrepreneurial companies would target if they're not kind of in the earliest of stages. And that's kind of where we would point you towards. It's a great growth goal for bootstrapped companies. Now, mature growth, 10 to 20%, I would kind of steer you away from that. That's fine for kind of legacy businesses that are really just sort of... you know, trying to die a slow and profitable death. Because if you're, you know, if you're only growing at about 10% a year, good chance you're going to get disrupted or you're in a market that is very, very, very slow.
Okay. So steady growth is where I would point most people, but for, you know, for all of our programs, these are the goals that we recommend top to bottom, which means I want to, you want to transform your current top line revenue into your future bankable profit. So where you sit right now, if you were to look back over the last 12 months, or what your run rating right now, what you basically did last month. Imagine if that wasn't just your revenue. Imagine if that much dropped to the bottom line. That's what we mean by top to bottom. Love that goal because it's just fun. But the minimum that I would encourage you to put in is double in three. At a bare minimum.
We're working with clients and we have folks in the founders' board at a minimum. We want to see you at least double your business. From a revenue and profitability perspective in three years. So we like top to bottom, but the reason we say that doubling three is a minimum is because it's very doable. If you just achieve 26% year-over-year growth, Three years in a row, you will double. Twenty-six percent. compounded year-over-year growth. is doubling in three years. 40% compounded year-over-year growth is doubling in two years. And 3% month over month is also doubling in two years, 3% compounded month over month growth. Is the math about the same as 40 you know year-over-year growth? So what I want you to think right now. As we get there. And again, we're almost done.
But what I want you to, I'm not going to put two minutes on the clock. I want you to think. Are you kind of thinking like more going top to bottom? Let's have our current top line revenue become bottom line or at a minimum double in three or something else. Okay, that's what I want you to be thinking right now. What does scale mean to you? Because we're talking about upgrading our operating system. Why? So that our business can scale without sacrificing our souls. Great. Awesome. What does scale mean to you? What does scale mean to your team? Answering this question is not just going to be the thing that motivates us. Moving forward, it's what's going to... create a lot of clarity as we move forward as well.
Now, the tool that we use to do this is a tool called the 12 Q planning grid, right? The 12 Q planning grid. And it's a tool that I happen to have up here right now. And Kyle, let's go ahead and drop the link. In if we haven't already done so. Because what I want us to do is begin to... You know, this is a tool that you can use to begin to kind of brainstorm this out. It's not a financial planning tool. It's not a budgeting tool or anything like that. Think about this as kind of like a dream calculator. All right, think about it like a dream calculator. Does everybody have access to this, Kyle? Yes, I dropped a link, a direct link to make a copy of it in the chat.
It can also be located in the Notion template under company goals. But again, there is a direct link to make a copy in the chat right now. Fantastic. So when you first go in, you'll see this initial tab. This is a blank one. And so this is the one that you will actually fill in. And the process is pretty simple. You would just simply write in. You know, what are your, you know, what are your kind of sales? And what we're looking at here is this: you know, what's kind of your annual sales? Right. What are you looking at in terms of annual sales? So that's what we're putting in here. In terms of annuals, you could say, like, what did I do last year? Right. Or, what did I do?
You know, what are we currently running at right now? So, if you did, you know, a hundred thousand dollars you know in revenue last month, you're running at 1. 2. So you just simply write, you know, 1. 2 million here. Or you can select an arbitrary starting point. I feel like right now, today, because maybe your business is a bit more seasonal. You know, we're doing about this much per year. If I were to ask you, how big is your company? How do we do about this much in revenue? That's your starting point. Okay. That is, that is your starting point. You would put that in here. And then you would also write in what your margin is. So what is your percent margin? And when you do that, I'll go over here.
You type that in, it'll calculate this for you. $2 million in revenue at a 5% margin means that you are uh, 100 doing a hundred thousand dollars in profit— maybe your margins are 10, 20, 30 percent. If you don't know, by the way. Most healthy companies are operating at between a 15% and a 25% profit margin. Some can be higher than that in the early days. But when you start getting into scale, 20% profit margin is a solid kind of place to point at. So you would simply fill this in. This is kind of where are you right now. And you can also change the date up here if you want to kind of say what your reference period was, or you don't have to, right? You can just delete that.
So we want to know our starting point. All right, we want to know a starting point, and then we want to say, okay. Three years from now. All right, three years from today, what we're going to do this year. We're going to do the next year. We're going to finish out that third year. At that point, how much do we generate? What is what we call the 12? cue target. 12Q, because again, we plan in three-year cycles. we execute in quarterly spreads. 12 Q is 12 quarters. okay. 12 quarters from today is the same as three years. okay. so we've got our starting point. Up here, we put our three-year target or our 12Q target. So where do you want to be in terms of revenue?
Now, if you want to double, then all you would simply do is say $2 million. Well, let's put $4 million here at the same profit margin, and that would double. If you're saying, no, no, I want to go, you know, top to bottom. Um, you might need to play with the margin number. Maybe you say, I think we could increase margin. So you increase the margin a little bit. Maybe you leave the margin the same and you kind of start increasing the sales until the profit number equals what you have here. So what you see in this area is a top to bottom. This is a top to bottom example. So sales of 2 million in three years, we want profit of 2 million. How do we do that?
Well, we need to increase from a 5% profit margin to a 20% profit margin. And that means that, you know, our sales are going to need to increase to 10 million as well. So that is, you know, that would be kind of a classic top to bottom figure. I'm going to answer any questions we have about this, but when you do this, I'll show you how this calculator works. What it will do then is it will calculate a straight line growth. So, if quarter over quarter, you were to just increase the same amount, it would say what that would need to be. And in this case, I believe that that 14% is kind of your quarter over quarter growth rate. And so you would see here.
You know, Q1, we're generating about this much right now. And. That's how much you need to increase in terms of sales and profit. This is what's going to give you that kind of dashed straight line. Then... What you could do is you can go over here and you can say, okay, let's put an actual projection. So on the projected, you can enter all these in because maybe you know that it's seasonal. Right. So maybe, you know, that, you know, in the first quarter, we don't do as well. And so while you would hope for 977, you think you're only going to do 721, something like that. So you could just put in general seasonal numbers. So this is where you can kind of begin to think out and project.
And maybe you only want to project out a few quarters. And then in the third column. This is where you can go in and say what actually happened. And the beautiful thing is if you do this, it'll begin to track, you know, for you, are we doing what we said we would do? And are we above or below our straight line number? So it's a really fun and interesting planning tool. But for now. What I want you to do is just begin to design. Top to bottom. Double in three. What is that for you? And then, if you want to go down on the projection side and make some projections with respect to sales and profit margin, you can certainly play with that as well.
And in terms of the actual, we leave that blank because we don't have a quarter together yet. To where you want to go without you having to sacrifice everything that you hold dear in the process to get it. All right, so just to close this out, and then I'm happy to answer any questions that came up. I got some action steps for you. Number one, finish drafting your 12-Q plan. And I do mean draft. Okay, draft it. You will finalize your 12Q plan. As a part of that very first two-day offsite that you do. So we're going to come back to this. Day one of your two-day off-site is a clarity day. And during the clarity day, one of the actions and activities that you will do— with your leadership team— will be. to create your 12-cube plan. Uh, plan.
Now part of the reason I have you do it now and then I have you do it later, is you're going to learn a lot about your business over the next eight to 12 weeks. The work that we do here together, you're going to learn, you may go back and look at the 12Q plan that you drafted here today. And go. That was completely wrong. We can grow so much more than that. Or, oh my God, what was I thinking? We got some pretty significant constraints that we're going to overcome. Whatever it is, it's just going to be fun to look back at that time. So I want you to draft it here. But you don't— I mean, if you've got a trusted member of your team that you want to share it with and involve them, a business partner, I think that's fine.
But don't roll this out to the entire company. Do not declare this as your three-year plan. Not yet. We will get there. That's part of the installation process. So if you were to add up here, you see in 12Q, because we put 10 million here. What it basically says is divides that by four. 6 that doesn't quite add up to 10 million uh so if you this is again again that's why i say this is not a financial projection tool um if you want to finish the year at 10 million then you would actually need to put in here like 10 . 5 or something like that, maybe even 11 million. Cause what we are looking at is a quarterly run rate because we're mostly interested in. what's quarter over quarter growth. So hopefully that answers and addresses that. So. Awesome stuff. Well, yes. Um, Simone who asked my company is only a team of two. Am I too small for the program? Oh, definitely not. Definitely not. I wish when I was a team of two. That I had done this. Um, it is once you have. You know, we like to say once you're generating. Our general rule of thumb, although exceptions are always made. Once you have a growth engine that is producing at least $10,000 a month in sales, at least three months in a row. Now's the time to start thinking about your operating system. Right. One growth engine, $10,000 a month in revenue. And you know you can depending on where you are in the world you may want to alter that a little bit.
But in general, for those in North America, one growth engine, $10,000 a month in sales, three months in a row. That means you got some predictable revenue coming through. And it's at about that point of predictable revenue. That's when it's time to start thinking about operating systems. Before you quote, unquote, need it. If you wait until you need it, then that's when you're going to flatline. And so, and so— no, I mean, I wish, again, it goes back to. You're the 16-year-old who's. investing, you know, saving and investing where the rest of us were spending it on, you know, video games and candy. Wishing that when we were 16, somebody would have told us to save and invest. To be fair, people did tell me to save and invest.
I just didn't listen. So I don't know. I think maybe that's part of it too— actually listening. Yeah, that is a part of it. That is definitely a part of it. So no, I wouldn't worry about it at all. Um, the only time where somebody is, um, where it might be tough is if, um, is if you literally just still have an idea. Um, that is definitely, that's definitely tough. Uh, what I would say there is focus on scalable growth. That particular accelerator, we're not going anywhere. We'll happily help you when you get here. But I've also seen people say, Um, I want to start a new business. So Jim, who kicked things off with the wind, talked about how he pivoted his business. That's one way to do it.
You can also say. We want to keep this current business, systemize it, build an operating system there, put an operator in to run it while we then go and build this new thing. And Jim didn't do that. I'm not suggesting, Jim, that you should have. But that's another way to think about this, people will say. You'll think about building an operating system for a brand new startup. You might want to think about doing it for an existing business to buy yourself the margin to get that built out. Awesome stuff. Um, well, thank you everybody for your participation again.
